title: He Kōrero o te Awa: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream | The Stories Our Waterway Holds
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore how the Mangakōtukutuku Stream (a significant local waterway whose name refers to the native pigeon/kererū) serves as a living text that holds stories of Ngāti Kahungunu identity, colonial impact, and environmental restoration. They will examine oral histories, ecological changes, and community voices connected to this awa.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare different text types (oral histories, written accounts, visual media) that document the cultural and ecological significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream.
Create a written narrative or digital story that weaves together mātauranga Māori perspectives and scientific observations about the awa.
Use effective language features and structures to communicate the importance of the stream to a specific audience (e.g., local council, community newsletter, school peers).
Participate actively and respectfully in a learning conversation with a local kaumatua or environmental kaitiaki, formulating relevant questions about the awa's history and future.

activities.title: He Titiro Whakamua, He Titiro Whakamuri | Looking to the Past to Understand the Present
activities.description: In pairs, students analyze a primary source photograph of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream from the early 1900s alongside a contemporary image. Using a graphic organizer, they identify and describe changes in the landscape, vegetation, and water quality, generating questions about what might have caused these changes.

activities.title: Whakarongo ki te Awa | Listening to the Waterway
activities.description: The class will listen to a pre-recorded short pūrākau (story) from a local kaumatua about the significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream as a source of food (tuna/eels, kōura/crayfish) and a spiritual pathway. Students will then identify key metaphors, emotions, and values conveyed in the oral text and discuss how storytelling preserves cultural knowledge.

activities.title: He Tuhinga Tohutoro | Writing a Place-Based Description
activities.description: Following a guided visit to a safe and accessible section of the stream bank, students will draft a rich, descriptive paragraph about the awa. They will be challenged to use sensory language (what they see, hear, smell) and incorporate at least two similes or metaphors that connect the stream's current state to its historical or cultural significance.

activities.title: Te Hāpai Ō | The Challenge and The Support
activities.description: Using provided articles from the Hawke's Bay Today archive and the regional council's environmental monitoring data, students work in small groups to create a simple T-chart. One side lists the historical and current pressures on the stream (e.g., pollution, land use changes). The other side lists the actions being taken to protect and restore it (e.g., community planting days, fencing projects).

activities.title: Ko Au te Awa, Ko te Awa Ko Au | I Am the River, and the River Is Me
activities.description: As a culminating creative task, students choose to either write a first-person narrative from the perspective of the stream or create a 'digital postcard' with an image and a powerful short text aimed at persuading the community to help protect the Mangakōtukutuku. These are shared in a classroom gallery walk.

resources: Local Kaumatua (e.g., from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi) for arranged virtual or in-class visit to share pūrākau
Hawke's Bay Knowledge Bank online archive for historical photographs and news clippings
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'TANK Group' reports and environmental data on local waterways
Digital voice recorders or tablets for recording observations and interviews

tasks: Completed graphic organizer comparing historical and contemporary images of the awa
Draft and final version of the descriptive paragraph using sensory and figurative language
Contribution to group T-chart analyzing pressures and protections for the stream
Final creative narrative or persuasive digital postcard

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
Science: Planet Earth and Beyond - Explain how the natural environment is changed by natural events and human actions.
